205	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
206	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
207	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
208	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
209	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
210	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
211	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
212	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
213	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
214	% SOURCE QUOTE:
215	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
216	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
217	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
218	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
219	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
220	%     intro Φ
221	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
222	%     infer_instance)"
223	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
224	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
225	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
226	\(A\).
227	
228	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e).} The substrate
229	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
230	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
231	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
232	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Hence
233	the monoidal structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
234	\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is obtained by applying
235	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i) ---
236	\emph{once} the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is supplied. That instance is
237	the \emph{sole genuinely-new obligation}: its two whiskering-stability fields are
238	\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, whose load-bearing local-injectivity half is
239	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, the only remaining open sorry. The
240	proof technique is that of template (iii) ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\):
241	a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, so
242	\((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an isomorphism
243	for \emph{any} \(F\) --- no flatness, no local triviality.
244	
245	What is \emph{genuinely Mathlib-absent} at the pinned commit: there is no
246	monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), and no \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)
247	stalk/fiber/point infrastructure (only \(\mathtt{Presheaf/ColimitFunctor}\)). So
248	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) for the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) sheafification
249	localizer must be built project-side, and the two stalk-level ingredients its
250	proof needs --- (d.1) the module-level stalkwise characterisation of \(J.W\) on
251	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
252	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor --- are
253	the residual ported by \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. Once
254	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) lands, the associator, unitors and braiding are
255	\emph{free} from (ii) and the bespoke three-step composite is superseded.
256	
257	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
258	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
259	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
260	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
261	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
262	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
263	independently of any upstream timing.
264	
265	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
266	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
267	
268	\begin{definition}
269	
270	\leanok
271	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
272	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
274	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
275	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
276	  \[
277	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
278	  \]
279	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
280	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
281	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
282	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
283	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
284	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
285	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
286	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
287	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
288	  canonical compatibility
289	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
290	     \;\cong\;
291	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
292	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
293	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
294	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
295	
296	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
297	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
298	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
299	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
300	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
301	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
302	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
303	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
304	\end{definition}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}
307	
308	\leanok
309	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
310	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
311	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
312	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
313	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
314	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
315	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
316	  \[
317	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
318	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
319	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
320	  \]
321	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
322	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
323	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
324	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
325	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
326	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
327	  \[
328	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
329	    \qquad
330	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
331	  \]
332	  the associator
333	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
334	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
335	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
336	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
337	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
342	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
343	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
344	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
345	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
346	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
347	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
348	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
349	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
350	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
351	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
352	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
353	\end{proof}
354	
355	\begin{remark}
356	[The full monoidal category is cheap via route~(e); the group law consumes only propositions]
357	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
358	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
359	        lem:whisker_of_W}
360	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
361	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
362	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
363	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
364	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
365	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. Under route~(e) this is
366	  \emph{cheap}, not expensive: it is exactly the output of
367	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
368	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
369	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), once the single instance
370	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) is supplied. All the
371	  coherence is then derived by the API.
372	
373	  Crucially, \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) needs \emph{no} closed structure. Its
374	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
375	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
376	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
377	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
378	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\) (tensoring with an isomorphism
379	  preserves isomorphisms). This is the same technique Mathlib uses for
380	  fixed-base presheaves in
381	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\), ported to
382	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). It uses neither
383	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
384	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
385	  false over a non-affine open and is therefore off the critical path); the
386	  earlier reading that arbitrary-\(F\) whisker stability requires
387	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) was the \emph{wrong altitude}.
388	
389	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
390	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
391	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
392	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
393	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
394	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
395	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
396	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
397	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
398	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, all now supplied by the route~(e) monoidal structure;
399	  none of them is built from the off-path restriction-compatibility isomorphism
400	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
401	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
402	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
403	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\): there
404	  over a fixed ring, here over the sheafification localizer via
405	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\).
406	\end{remark}
407	
408	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
409	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
410	
411	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
412	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
413	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
414	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
415	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
416	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
417	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes ---
418	under route~(e) these are read off the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure of
419	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} --- together with the closure of \(\otimes\) on the
420	locally-trivial \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype
421	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and the interaction with the pullback
422	functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
423	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is retained as an off-path supplement, not as a
424	group-law ingredient; cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
425	
426	\begin{lemma}
427	
428	\leanok
429	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
430	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
431	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
432	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
433	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
434	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
435	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
436	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
437	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
438	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
439	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
440	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
441	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
442	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
443	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
444	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
445	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
446	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
447	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
448	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
449	  \[
450	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
451	      \;\;:=\;\;
452	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
453	  \]
454	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
455	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
456	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
457	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
458	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
459	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
460	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
461	\end{lemma}
462	
463	\begin{proof}
464	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
465	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
466	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
467	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
468	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
469	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
470	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
471	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
472	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
473	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
474	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
475	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
476	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
477	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
478	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
479	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
480	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
481	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
482	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
483	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
484	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
485	
486	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
487	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
488	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
489	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
490	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
491	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
492	\end{proof}
493	
494	\begin{lemma}
495	
496	\leanok
497	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
498	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
500	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
501	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
502	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
503	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
504	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
505	  morphism
506	  \[
507	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
508	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
509	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
510	  \]
511	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
512	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
513	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
514	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
515	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
516	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
517	\end{lemma}
518	
519	\begin{proof}
520	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
521	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
522	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
523	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
524	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
525	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
526	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
527	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
528	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
529	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
530	  isomorphism}
531	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
532	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
533	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
534	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
535	  therefore extension of scalars along
536	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
537	
538	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
539	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
540	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
541	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
542	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
543	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
544	  the abstract pullback functor.
545	
546	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
547	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
548	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
549	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
550	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
551	   \;\cong\;
552	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
553	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
554	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
555	  comparison
556	  \[
557	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
558	      \;\longrightarrow\;
559	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
560	      \;\otimes\;
561	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
562	  \]
563	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
564	
565	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
566	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
567	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
568	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
569	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
570	  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification
571	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) via
572	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
573	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
574	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
575	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
576	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
577	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
578	  coherence isomorphisms
579	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
580	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
581	  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.
582	\end{proof}
583	
584	\begin{lemma}
585	
586	\leanok
587	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
588	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
589	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
590	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
591	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
592	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
593	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
594	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
595	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
596	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
597	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
598	\end{lemma}
599	
600	\begin{proof}
601	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
602	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
603	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
604	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
605	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
606	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
607	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
608	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
609	\end{proof}
610	
611	\begin{lemma}
612	
613	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
614	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
615	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
616	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
617	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
618	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
619	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
620	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
621	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
622	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
623	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
624	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
625	  left-whiskered morphism
626	  \[
627	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
628	  \]
629	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
630	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
631	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
632	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
633	
634	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
635	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
636	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
637	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
638	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
639	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
640	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
641	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
642	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
643	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
644	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
645	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
646	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
647	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
648	\end{lemma}
649	
650	\begin{proof}
651	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
652	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
653	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
654	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
655	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
656	
657	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
658	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
659	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
660	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
661	  \[
662	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
663	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
664	  \]
665	
666	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
667	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
668	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
669	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
670	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
671	
672	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
673	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
674	  linear maps (Mathlib
675	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
676	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
677	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
678	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
679	
680	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
681	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
682	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
683	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
684	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
685	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
686	\end{proof}
687	
688	\begin{lemma}
689	
690	\leanok
691	[Sheafification inverts a localizer morphism of presheaves of modules]
692	  \label{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
693	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W}
694	  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
695	  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
696	  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
697	  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
698	  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
699	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
700	  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
701	  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
702	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
703	  isomorphism:
704	  \[
705	    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
706	    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}
707	  \]
708	  This is a closed go/no-go bridge --- it converts ``\,a morphism lies in
709	  \(J.W\)\,'' into ``\,its sheafification is invertible\,''. Under route~(e) the
710	  associator is the API-derived one (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), so this bridge is
711	  retained as a supplement rather than as the associator's load-bearing step.
712	\end{lemma}
713	
714	\begin{proof}
715	  This is a one-morphism reading of the structural identity that the
716	  sheafification of presheaves of \(R_0\)-modules \emph{is} the localization of
717	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R_0\) at the class
718	  \(J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0)\) of morphisms whose
719	  underlying map of presheaves of abelian groups is locally bijective. Concretely,
720	  the underlying \(\mathtt{AddCommGrp}\)-valued sheafification inverts exactly the
721	  locally bijective morphisms, and Mathlib's
722	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.inverseImage\_W\_toPresheaf\_eq\_inverseImage\_%
723	  isomorphisms}\) identifies the preimage of \(J.W\) under
724	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0\) with the preimage of the class of isomorphisms under
725	  the module-level sheafification functor. Reading that equality of morphism
726	  classes at the single morphism \(f\): the hypothesis
727	  \(J.W\,\bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\bigr)\) places \(f\) in
728	  that preimage, hence \((\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f\) is an
729	  isomorphism. No new construction is required beyond instantiating the existing
730	  Mathlib identity at one morphism; this lemma is a closed thin wrapper, retained
731	  as a supplement under route~(e).
732	\end{proof}

734	\section{Route~(e): the \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal structure}
735	\label{sec:tensorobj_route_e}
736	
737	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
738	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
739	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
740	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
741	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
742	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
743	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
744	whiskering-stability lemmas below. Their load-bearing local-injectivity half is
745	the sole remaining open obligation of the entire group-law engine.
746	
747	\begin{lemma}
748	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
749	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
750	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
751	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
752	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
753	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
754	  bijective morphisms). Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
755	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
756	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
757	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
758	  left-whiskered morphism
759	  \[
760	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
761	  \]
762	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
763	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
764	
765	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
766	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), and it is
767	  \emph{the sole remaining open obligation for the whole group-law engine}: every
768	  other ingredient of route~(e) is either supplied by Mathlib or closed
769	  project-side.
770	\end{lemma}
771	
772	\begin{proof}
773	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
774	  The argument is the stalkwise one used by Mathlib for fixed-base presheaves in
775	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
776	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
777	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
778	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
779	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
780	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
781	  \[
782	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
783	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
784	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
785	  \]
786	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
787	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
788	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
789	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
790	
791	  The two ingredients this stalkwise computation needs are, at the pinned commit,
792	  absent from Mathlib at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level, and constitute
793	  the genuine residual:
794	  \begin{enumerate}
795	    \item[(d.1)] the stalkwise characterisation of the module-level \(J.W\) on
796	      \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) (that \(g \in J.W\) is equivalent to \(g_x\) being an
797	      isomorphism for every \(x\)); and
798	    \item[(d.2)] the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x \otimes_{R_x}
799	      B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
800	      module-presheaf tensor (using that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) /
801	      \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered colimits over a module category).
802	  \end{enumerate}
803	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal
804	  concrete category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1)+(d.2)
805	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work.
806	\end{proof}
807	
808	\begin{lemma}
809	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
810	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
811	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_W, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_W}
812	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
813	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
814	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
815	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
816	  \(J.W\):
817	  \[
818	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
819	    \qquad
820	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
821	  \]
822	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
823	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
824	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
825	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
826	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
827	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
828	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
829	\end{lemma}
830	
831	\begin{proof}
832	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
833	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
834	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
835	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
836	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
837	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
838	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
839	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
840	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
841	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
842	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
843	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
844	\end{proof}
845	
846	\begin{lemma}
847	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
848	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
849	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
850	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
851	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
852	  instance
853	  \[
854	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
855	  \]
856	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
857	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
858	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
859	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
860	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
861	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
862	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
863	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
864	  category
865	  \[
866	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
867	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
868	  \]
869	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
870	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
871	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
872	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
873	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
874	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
875	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
876	  composites.
877	
878	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it is currently
879	  \emph{stated-but-unformalized} (no Lean declaration realizes the
880	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance yet), pending the sole open obligation
881	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
882	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
883	\end{lemma}
884	
885	\begin{proof}
886	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
887	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
888	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
889	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
890	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
891	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
892	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
893	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
894	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
895	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
896	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
897	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
898	  at any point.
899	\end{proof}

905	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}

987	[Braiding for $\otimes_X$]

1225	  then \(\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
1226	  \mathcal{O}_X)\).}
1227	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
1228	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1229	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
1230	  \[
1231	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
1232	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1233	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
1234	  \]
1235	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
1236	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
1237	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
1238	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
1239	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
1240	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
1241	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
1242	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
1243	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
1244	  \(\otimes_X\).
1245	\end{definition}
1246	
1247	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
1248	  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
1249	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
1250	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1251	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:jw_ismonoidal,
1252	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1253	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1254	  % modules), Definition 01CX (definition-pic) + Lemma lemma-constructions-
1255	  % invertible (tensor of invertibles is invertible; dual is the inverse)
1256	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200-L4213 and L4350-L4357).
1257	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1258	  % "\begin{lemma}
1259	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
1260	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1261	  % \begin{enumerate}
1262	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{N}$ are invertible
1263	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then so is
1264	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$.
1265	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
1266	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
1267	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1268	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
1269	  % is an isomorphism.
1270	  % \end{enumerate}
1271	  % \end{lemma}
1272	  % [\dots]
1273	  % \begin{definition}
1274	  % \label{definition-pic}
1275	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1276	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
1277	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
1278	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
1279	  % corresponding to tensor product.
1280	  % \end{definition}"
1281	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1282	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1283	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1284	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1285	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1286	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1287	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1288	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1289	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1290	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1291	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1292	  01CR).
1293	
1294	  Route~(e) supplies this directly. By \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} the category
1295	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
1296	  is a (symmetric) \(\MonoidalCategory\) with tensor \(\otimes_X\) and unit
1297	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), obtained from \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\).
1298	  Its set of isomorphism classes --- the skeleton \(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.%
1299	  \mathtt{Modules}\,X)\) --- is therefore a commutative monoid under
1300	  \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\): the multiplication is well-defined because
1301	  \(\otimes_X\) is a bifunctor (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality});
1302	  the unit is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and associativity, unitality and commutativity
1303	  are the existence of the coherence isomorphisms
1304	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1305	  read as equalities of iso-classes. The Picard group is then the group of
1306	  \emph{units} of this commutative monoid,
1307	  \[
1308	    \Pic(X)
1309	      \;\;=\;\;
1310	    \mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X)\bigr),
1311	  \]
1312	  exactly as Mathlib builds
1313	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}
1314	  \,R))\) and transports \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). A class \([M]\) is a unit
1315	  precisely when \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
1316	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse being carried
1317	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\). The pinned carrier
1318	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) is this commutative monoid of all
1319	  iso-classes; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at
1320	  the pinned commit.
1321	
1322	  Because the route~(e) associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is natural in
1323	  \emph{arbitrary} objects (it is the associator of the
1324	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) structure, not a locally-trivial-scoped
1325	  composite), the group law needs \emph{no} auxiliary bridge from
1326	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): the coherence
1327	  isomorphisms apply to the invertible objects directly.
1328	\end{lemma}
1329	
1330	\begin{proof}
1331	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1332	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:jw_ismonoidal,
1333	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1334	  By \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} the substrate \(\otimes_X\) is the tensor of a
1335	  monoidal structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with unit
1336	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). Its skeleton --- the set of isomorphism classes --- is a
1337	  commutative monoid under \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\): multiplication is
1338	  well-defined by the bifunctoriality of
1339	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, and the monoid axioms are
1340	  the existence of the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1341	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and (commutativity) the braiding
1342	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, read as equalities of iso-classes; no pentagon,
1343	  triangle or hexagon identity is invoked, since each axiom asserts only the
1344	  \emph{existence} of an isomorphism. Passing to units: a class \([M]\) is
1345	  invertible in this monoid precisely when \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible, with
1346	  inverse \([N]\) for the witness \(N\) of \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
1347	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). Hence
1348	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\) is an abelian
1349	  group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- assembled exactly by
1350	  the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
1351	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\).
1352	\end{proof}

1354	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
1355	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
1356	
1357	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
1358	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
1359	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
1360	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1361	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
1362	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
1363	
1364	\begin{theorem}
1365	
1366	\leanok
1367	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
1368	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
1369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
1370	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1371	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1372	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1373	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1374	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1375	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
1376	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
1377	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1378	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1379	
1380	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
1381	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
1382	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
1383	  % groups)
1384	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
1385	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1386	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
1387	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
1388	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
1389	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
1390	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
1391	  % \end{dfn}
1392	  %  [\dots]
1393	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
1394	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
1395	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
1396	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
1397	  % topologies  by
1398	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
1399	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1400	  %  \end{dfn}"
1401	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
1402	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
1403	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1404	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1405	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1406	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1407	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1408	  \[
1409	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1410	  \]
1411	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1412	  abelian-group structure with addition
1413	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1414	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1415	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1416	  inverse
1417	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1418	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1419	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1420	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1421	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1422	  map
1423	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1424	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1425	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1426	\end{theorem}
1427	
1428	\begin{proof}
1429	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1430	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1431	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1432	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1433	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1434	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1435	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1436	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1437	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1438	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1439	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1440	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1441	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1442	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1443	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1444	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1445	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1446	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1447	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1448	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1449	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1450	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1451	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1452	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1453	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1454	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1455	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1456	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1457	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1458	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1459	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1460	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1461	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1462	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1463	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1464	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1465	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1466	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1467	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1468	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1469	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1470	
1471	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1472	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1473	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1474	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1475	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1476	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1477	
1478	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1479	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1480	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1481	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1482	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1483	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1484	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1485	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1486	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1487	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1488	\end{proof}

2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}

9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification

20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},

34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
166	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
167	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by
170	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
174	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
175	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[
178	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
179	    \qquad
180	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
181	  \]
182	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
183	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
184	  the pullback maps \(g^\sharp\) of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}

207	\begin{lemma}\leanok
208	[Naturality respects the group structure]
209	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
213	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
214	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
215	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
216	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
217	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
220	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
221	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
222	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
223	  %  [\dots]
224	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
225	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
226	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
227	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
228	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
229	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
230	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
231	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
232	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
233	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
234	  composition laws \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
235	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp\) hold as identities of group
236	  homomorphisms.
237	\end{lemma}
238	
239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
240	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
241	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
242	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
243	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
244	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial at b80f227 carries a
245	% zero AddMonoidHom body (\(0 : \dots \to \dots\)), i.e.\ the zero map
246	% between two trivial-group endpoints, NOT the pullback-descended
247	% homomorphism this lemma describes. Moreover, because the codomain's
248	% \texttt{Zero} instance is derived from the sorry-body
249	% \texttt{addCommGroup} (the file-local genuine sorry of the chapter),
250	% the closure also inherits \texttt{sorryAx} via that typeclass leak;
251	% it is therefore not even axiom-clean. The closure is gated on the
252	% upstream Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap. DO
253	% NOT promote this block to \leanok via the deterministic
254	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in
255	% review, until the body is replaced with the mathematically correct
256	% map descended from \(g_C^*\).
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
260	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
261	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
262	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
263	  structure sheaf, hence the map
264	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
265	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
266	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
267	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
268	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
269	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
270	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
271	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
272	\end{proof}

291	  together with the structure maps \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) of

305	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf at b80f227 is defined as
306	% \texttt{PicSharp \_C}, i.e.\ it re-exports the constant-PUnit-functor

317	  The object map is well defined by

319	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is an abelian group. The morphism map

327	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
328	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
329	
330	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
331	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
332	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
333	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
334	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
335	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
336	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
337	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
338	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
339	
340	\begin{definition}\leanok
341	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
342	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
344	  % NOTE (iter-176 review): the iter-176 Lane G prover renamed this declaration
345	  % from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`` to
346	  % ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf`` to avoid a name collision with
347	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
348	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
349	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
350	  % below is also corrected.
351	  
352	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
353	  % Definition df:Pfs --- the second half of df:Pfs names the \'etale sheaf
354	  % associated to the relative Picard functor
355	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318
356	  %  and the surrounding paragraph L1320-L1328).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
359	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
360	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
361	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
362	  % topologies  by
363	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
364	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
365	  %  \end{dfn}
366	  %  [\dots]
367	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
368	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
369	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
370	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
371	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
372	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
373	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
374	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
375	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
376	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
377	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
378	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
379	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
380	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):
381	  \[
382	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;
383	       \bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\bigr)^{\sim_{\et}}.
384	  \]
385	  Equivalently, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is the unique (up to canonical
386	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)
387	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
388	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
389	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
390	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
391	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
392	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
393	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
394	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
395	\end{definition}

498	\begin{proof}
499	
500	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
501	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical
502	  sheafification unit) supplies, in particular, a morphism of
503	  presheaves \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); the
504	  hom-set of presheaf morphisms between these two objects is therefore
505	  nonempty. The full universal property of this canonical morphism

507	  the sheafification is uniquely pinned by it --- is the content of
508	  the forward-looking \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
509	  below; see its proof sketch for the canonical formulation.
